patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 42-year-old anxious, vague-speaking man. Start with: “I’ve been getting really shaky and foggy, and my hands and feet keep tingling.” Volunteer: hand tremor when holding/writing, feeling on-edge/irritable, poor concentration/forgetfulness, more headaches, occasional unsteady walking. If asked about other symptoms, mention: sore/bleeding gums with metallic taste, intermittent cramps/nausea, on-and-off diarrhea, occasional blurry focus, muffled hearing, pins-and-needles worse at night, fatigue, and that an MRI showed “white matter changes.” Do NOT volunteer: less urine, ankle puffiness, foamy urine, taste/smell changes, sweating/palpitations, insomnia unless directly asked. Be resistant about exposures: only admit skin-lightening cream and handling liquid metal at home if the clinician asks specifically and reassures you; minimize details due to fear of job/legal trouble. Express misconceptions: blame diabetes low sugar, epilepsy twitching, stress, or B12 deficiency; ask for a quick fix to keep working; doubt affordability and prefer OTC “natural detox.” If met with empathy, soften, share more, and ask what tests are truly necessary and cheapest.

medications: Bevacizumab 10 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks
Insulin glargine 24 units subcutaneous nightly
Insulin aspart 8 units subcutaneous with meals (titrate per carbohydrate intake/correction scale)
Levetiracetam 1000 mg PO twice daily
Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
confounders: Bevacizumab-associated hypertension and proteinuria (can mimic/worsen renal findings seen with mercury toxicity)
Diabetic kidney disease (albuminuria and reduced eGFR complicate attribution of renal injury)
Epilepsy with antiseizure medication effects (tremor, dizziness, cognitive slowing can overlap with neurotoxicity)
Occupational exposure history uncertainty (possible intermittent exposure from artisanal metalwork/thermometer cleanup)
MRI white matter changes can be multifactorial (toxic leukoencephalopathy vs vascular small-vessel disease from diabetes/hypertension)

acceptable_alternatives: Toxic leukoencephalopathy due to heavy metal exposure (mercury)
Chronic mercury exposure with CNS white matter changes
required_evidence: Elevated mercury level in whole blood and/or urine above reference range, consistent with exposure timing (blood reflects recent exposure; urine reflects inorganic/elemental burden)
Neurologic involvement with imaging showing abnormal cerebral white matter morphology consistent with toxic leukoencephalopathy
Exclusion of major alternative heavy metals with similar neurotoxicity (normal/low blood lead; no significant inorganic arsenic elevation)
History suggestive of exposure source (e.g., occupational, household, skin-lightening creams, broken thermometer, artisanal mining), if obtainable

expected_lab_results.Whole blood mercury: Elevated above reference range; supports recent exposure (often more elevated in organic methylmercury exposure but may be elevated with significant inorganic/elemental exposure)
expected_lab_results.Urine mercury (24-hour): Elevated above reference range; supports inorganic/elemental mercury exposure and body burden
expected_lab_results.Urine mercury (spot, creatinine-correcte: Elevated above reference range; correlates with 24-hour urine and useful for monitoring trend
expected_lab_results.Blood lead level: Within reference range or not elevated (helps exclude lead as primary cause of white matter changes)
expected_lab_results.Arsenic (urine, inorganic): Not elevated (helps exclude inorganic arsenic toxicity)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Baseline value to assess renal function prior to chelation; may be normal or elevated depending on kidney involvement
expected_lab_results.BUN: Baseline value to assess renal function; may be normal or elevated
expected_lab_results.eGFR (CKD-EPI): Documented to guide chelator choice/dosing; may be normal or reduced

medications: Dimercaprol (BAL) 3 mg/kg IM every 4 hours for 2 days, then every 6 hours for 1 day, then every 12 hours for 10 days (use for severe symptomatic inorganic mercury poisoning; consult toxicology)

Succimer (DMSA) 10 mg/kg PO every 8 hours for 5 days, then 10 mg/kg PO every 12 hours for 14 days (for mild-moderate symptomatic mercury poisoning or step-down after BAL; dosing per weight; consult toxicology)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever (supportive)
non_pharmacological: Immediate identification and cessation of mercury exposure; remove/avoid source and involve occupational/environmental health as indicated
Contact regional Poison Control Center/medical toxicology for chelation indications, agent selection, and monitoring plan
Hospitalize if significant neurologic symptoms, rapidly progressive deficits, or severe elevations; monitor neurologic status
Avoid seafood/high-mercury fish until exposure type clarified; if exposure suspected to be methylmercury, emphasize dietary source elimination and toxicology guidance
Supportive care: hydration, symptom control; consider neurology consultation for toxic leukoencephalopathy evaluation and MRI correlation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck urine (spot creatinine-corrected or 24-hour) and/or whole blood mercury in 1-2 weeks after exposure removal/chelation initiation, then every 2-4 weeks until clearly declining and symptoms stabilize; renal function (BUN/Cr/eGFR) within 48-72 hours of starting chelation and at least weekly during therapy.

must_include: Mercury can affect the brain and cause white matter changes and neurologic symptoms
Explain what the mercury tests mean (blood for recent exposure; urine for inorganic/elemental burden) and that trends matter
Discuss key alternative causes being checked (lead, inorganic arsenic) and why
First step is stopping exposure and removing the source
Chelation may be recommended depending on level, symptoms, and exposure type; it binds mercury to help eliminate it
Need for monitoring kidney function and repeat mercury levels during/after treatment

Potential adverse effects of chelators (GI upset, rash, neutropenia/hepatotoxicity with succimer; hypertension/tachycardia/nausea and injection-site pain with dimercaprol) and when to seek urgent care
Set expectations: neurologic recovery can be slow; some symptoms may persist depending on exposure duration and severity
Encourage questions about exposure sources (workplace/home products/foods) and involve public health resources when relevant
Reinforce that penicillin allergy does not affect chelation choice but should remain documented
Avoid suspected exposure sources (certain fish with high mercury, skin-lightening creams, occupational vapors) until clarified
Hand hygiene and safe cleanup guidance if household spill suspected (do not vacuum mercury; contact local hazardous materials resources)
Importance of follow-up testing and keeping appointments to ensure levels are decreasing

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit exposure history (occupational/home/dietary), symptom timeline, neuro/renal/GI symptoms, pregnancy status, and risk of ongoing exposure; identify red flags needing urgent care.

Order appropriate mercury testing (blood for recent exposure; urine for inorganic/elemental burden) plus baseline safety labs (CBC, CMP/creatinine, urinalysis) and alternative/toxicology evaluation (lead level, inorganic arsenic) to support diagnosis and rule out key alternatives.
Interpret results with attention to specimen type, exposure type, units, and trends; determine severity and whether chelation is indicated.
Make or confirm diagnosis of mercury poisoning/toxicity (or suspected mercury exposure) and document differential (lead, arsenic) and rationale.
Perform allergy check prior to prescribing; document that penicillin allergy does not alter chelator selection but remains important.
Assess for drug interactions prior to prescribing chelation (e.g., with current meds/supplements) and adjust plan if needed.

If indicated, prescribe appropriate chelation (e.g., succimer for many inorganic cases; dimercaprol for severe cases per context) only after contraindication assessment; otherwise focus on exposure cessation and monitoring.
Communicate diagnosis, testing meaning, treatment rationale/risks, exposure avoidance and safe cleanup, expected recovery course, and when to seek urgent care.
Arrange follow-up for repeat mercury levels and renal/hepatic/CBC monitoring during/after treatment, symptom reassessment, and coordination with occupational/public health resources.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly distinguishes mercury test interpretation: blood mercury reflects recent exposure; urine mercury reflects inorganic/elemental burden; emphasizes that trends over time matter more than a single value.
Agent explains mercury neurotoxicity, including that mercury can affect the brain and cause white matter changes and neurologic symptoms.
Agent discusses key alternative causes being checked (lead and inorganic arsenic) and provides a rationale for testing them.
Agent prioritizes exposure cessation/removal of source as first-line management before pharmacologic intervention.
Agent describes chelation conceptually (binds mercury to enhance elimination) and states chelation decisions depend on level, symptoms, and exposure type.

Agent performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and a contraindication check; contraindication check is treated as critical (no prescribing without it).
Agent avoids recommending chelation for low-level/asymptomatic cases without clear indication; offers monitoring and exposure control instead.
Agent includes monitoring needs during/after treatment: kidney function and repeat mercury levels; also mentions CBC/LFT monitoring when using succimer.

Agent counsels on chelator adverse effects and urgent return precautions: succimer (GI upset, rash, neutropenia/hepatotoxicity); dimercaprol (hypertension, tachycardia, nausea, injection-site pain) and when to seek urgent care (e.g., severe rash, fever, jaundice, bleeding, chest pain, severe headache, worsening neuro symptoms).

minimal_paths.description: Suspected or mild mercury exposure/toxicity where symptoms are mild or absent and levels do not clearly warrant chelation; focus on stopping exposure, confirmatory testing, and monitoring.
must_collect: Exposure source and timing (fish/occupational vapor/skin-lightening creams/household spill)
Neurologic symptoms (tremor, paresthesias, cognitive/behavioral change, headache)
Red flags (severe neuro deficits, chest pain, severe hypertension, inability to ambulate, altered mental status)
steps: ASK: focused exposure + symptom + medication/allergy + pregnancy + red flags history
ORDER_LAB: blood mercury, urine mercury (spot with creatinine or 24h per local practice), CBC, CMP/creatinine, urinalysis; lead level; inorganic arsenic
GET_RESULTS: interpret by specimen and exposure type; assess need for urgent escalation
DIAGNOSE: suspected mercury exposure/toxicity vs alternative heavy metal toxicity; document differential
EDUCATE: stop exposure/remove source; explain tests and trends; discuss alternatives being checked; lifestyle avoidance and spill safety; recovery expectations
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: repeat mercury levels and renal function; consider occupational/public health referral; provide safety-net instructions

minimal_paths.description: Confirmed mercury toxicity with symptoms and/or levels consistent with benefit from chelation (typically inorganic/elemental exposure) after contraindication assessment; initiate chelation plus monitoring and exposure control.
must_collect: Exposure source and type (elemental vapor vs inorganic vs organic/fish)
Symptom severity and progression (neuro, renal, GI)
Timing of last exposure and whether exposure is ongoing
Ability to adhere to follow-up and monitoring
steps: ASK: detailed exposure characterization + symptom severity + comorbidities + meds/allergies + pregnancy + red flags
ORDER_LAB: blood mercury, urine mercury, CBC, CMP/creatinine (± LFTs), urinalysis; lead level; inorganic arsenic
GET_RESULTS: confirm pattern consistent with inorganic/elemental burden; assess severity and kidney function
DIAGNOSE: mercury poisoning/toxicity; document exposure type and differential
CHECK_ALLERGY: verify/document allergies; note penicillin allergy not relevant to chelation selection
CHECK_INTERACTION: evaluate interactions with current meds/supplements
PRESCRIBE: chelation regimen appropriate to severity/context; ensure contraindication assessment completed prior to ordering
EDUCATE: exposure cessation first; explain chelation mechanism/indication; monitoring plan; adverse effects and urgent precautions; discuss alternatives being checked
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: repeat mercury levels and renal function (and CBC/LFTs as applicable) during/after therapy; arrange occupational/public health resources

acceptable_variants.description: Poison control/toxicology involvement integrated into education and follow-up planning (without a dedicated tool), especially for uncertain exposure type or severe symptoms.
steps: EDUCATE (includes contacting poison control/toxicology and public health guidance)
PRESCRIBE (only if indicated and contraindication assessed)

acceptable_variants.description: Education delivered in two passes (initial counseling after history, then detailed counseling after results/diagnosis), as long as all communication milestones are met.
steps: EDUCATE (initial exposure cessation and spill safety)

acceptable_variants.description: If chelation is not indicated, safety checks may be omitted because no prescribing occurs; the agent must still document rationale and monitoring plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old woman, anxious but clear and cooperative. You are the child’s mother and start with: “He’s been having more angry outbursts at school and his teacher says he can’t focus.” Volunteer early that he’s always been behind in learning and needs extra help, he snores loudly and sometimes seems to stop breathing at night, and he’s picky with food and often constipated.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: choking/coughing with thin liquids, sensory sensitivities, high pain tolerance, morning headaches/tiredness, dental crowding/cavities and dental-visit struggles.

Resist/disclose only if pressed: public genital touching when stressed, daytime accidents/bedwetting, bullying.

Mention your worry PANDAS after recent sore throat; describe possible tics and “daydreaming.” Express misconceptions that genetic testing is always unaffordable/not covered, working will automatically end benefits, and more diagnoses could raise insurance costs. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more details.

Syndromic phenotype consistent with Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome (e.g., characteristic facial features, broad thumbs/halluces, developmental delay/intellectual disability, growth issues)
Exclusion of alternative etiologies when clinically indicated (e.g., chromosomal microarray for 16p13.3 microdeletion if not already done)

If iron deficiency is suspected/possible, order CBC with indices and ferritin (± iron studies) to confirm before iron therapy; aligns with treatment milestone.
Retrieve lab results to determine whether iron deficiency is present and guide dosing/duration counseling.
State Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome due to CREBBP mutation when supported; explicitly note genetic confirmation and address differential diagnoses and why they fit/ do not fit.
Explain diagnosis, prognosis variability, and management approach (developmental supports and screening). Provide lifestyle/therapy guidance and address family concerns.
If prescribing iron or other meds, check for drug interactions (e.g., calcium/antacids, levothyroxine) as a non-critical safety check.
If iron deficiency is confirmed, prescribe iron with correct pediatric dosing framework and administration guidance; ensure allergy and contraindication checks are completed first.
Arrange follow-up for monitoring development, associated-condition screening, and reassessment of iron response (repeat labs) and tolerability.
Close encounter after ensuring questions answered, red flags reviewed, and follow-up plan established.

nl_assertions: Explains Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome as a genetic condition most often due to CREBBP changes and clarifies that diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing (not solely by appearance).

Provides a concise differential and explicitly contrasts: (1) 16p13.3 microdeletion as an alternative genetic mechanism; (2) polymicrogyria considered if neuroimaging/neurologic signs suggest; (3) PANDAS requires abrupt OCD/tics temporally related to strep infection and does not explain a congenital syndrome.

States there is no single curative medication; management focuses on developmental therapies, school supports, and screening for associated medical issues (e.g., cardiac, vision/hearing, sleep-disordered breathing, growth/nutrition).

If iron deficiency is present, gives appropriate iron counseling: dosing approach in elemental iron, best taken with vitamin C-containing beverage, avoid co-administration with calcium/antacids, expected side effects (GI upset, constipation, dark stools), and expected duration with plan for lab recheck.

Completes critical safety checks before prescribing: reviews allergies (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and performs contraindication consideration; documents how these affect future care (avoid culprit beta-lactam combination unless evaluated; latex precautions).
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing when applicable (non-critical) and adjusts administration timing to reduce interactions (e.g., separate from calcium/antacids/thyroid meds).
Addresses caregiver concerns and sets prognosis expectations: variable developmental outcomes, benefit of early intervention, and importance of regular follow-up and coordinated care.

Provides lifestyle education: consistent therapy participation and school-based supports; nutrition counseling for picky eating/feeding issues and iron-rich diet if suspected/confirmed deficiency; sleep hygiene and evaluation for sleep-disordered breathing if symptoms present.
Avoids unsafe or unsupported claims (e.g., promising normal development or curative treatments) and uses clear, family-centered language with teach-back or confirmation of understanding.

minimal_paths.description: Patient with confirmed CREBBP-related Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome and labs consistent with iron deficiency; agent confirms diagnosis, provides comprehensive counseling, and initiates iron therapy safely.
steps: DIAGNOSE (Rubinstein-Taybi due to CREBBP; address differential including 16p13.3 microdeletion, polymicrogyria, PANDAS)
EDUCATE (no curative med; therapies/screening; prognosis; lifestyle and school supports; sleep evaluation if indicated)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (recheck labs and symptoms; monitor constipation/tolerance; coordinate therapies)

minimal_paths.description: Confirmed CREBBP-related Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome; iron studies are normal or not indicated; agent focuses on counseling, screening, and follow-up without prescribing iron.
steps: EDUCATE (diagnosis confirmation; differential; management/screening; prognosis; lifestyle/school supports; sleep hygiene and evaluate SDB if symptomatic)

minimal_paths.description: Clinical features suggest Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome but genetic confirmation is pending/absent; agent explains need for genetic testing, addresses differential, and provides supportive management guidance.
steps: EDUCATE (explain suspected diagnosis; recommend/outline genetic testing confirmation; discuss differential including 16p13.3 microdeletion, polymicrogyria, PANDAS)
ORDER_LAB (only if clinically indicated for anemia/iron deficiency risk)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (genetics referral/testing results review; screening plan)

steps: EDUCATE (include iron-rich diet guidance and what results will mean)

general_concerns: Worries that most symptoms mean something serious like cancer, especially if they come and go.
Believes that if a test is normal once, it means the problem is 'all in my head' and doctors won’t take it seriously.
Thinks antibiotics help most illnesses and may ask for them 'just in case' even without clear signs of infection.
Assumes high blood pressure or blood sugar always causes obvious symptoms, so if she feels okay it must not be an issue.
Believes stress alone can permanently 'damage the heart' and that feeling palpitations means a heart attack is starting.
Thinks imaging (CT/MRI) is always the best way to find the cause and that blood tests are less reliable.
economic_concerns: Fears missing shifts and losing hours if more appointments or tests are needed.
Worries about surprise bills and prefers the 'cheapest test' even if it may not answer the clinical question.
Thinks generic medications are lower quality and may not work as well, but also worries she can’t afford brand-name drugs.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 47-year-old anxious grocery shift supervisor. Start with: “I’ve had this burning when I pee and I keep needing to go all the time.” Be tangential—mention work stress, headaches, and being worried about missing shifts or surprise bills. Volunteer: burning with urination, frequent small amounts, urgency/rushing, and lower belly pressure. If asked, add: strong-smelling cloudy urine, one episode of pinkish urine, mild feverish/chills last night, one-sided low back pain, sex uncomfortable lately, and fatigue. Do NOT volunteer unless directly asked: holding pee at work, low water intake, new scented soap/bubble bath, uncertain wiping, and past similar episode where you stopped antibiotics early. Resist disclosing unless asked about sex/STIs/vaginal symptoms: new partner, inconsistent condom use, unusual discharge, fear it’s an STI, and using “feminine wash” internally. Misconceptions to express: think it’s yeast/stress; cranberry/vitamin C not helping; worry generic meds won’t work but brand is too expensive. If clinician is empathic, you soften, admit embarrassment, and ask for the cheapest necessary tests and a plan that won’t risk your job.

medications: metformin ER 1000 mg PO twice daily
lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO daily
sertraline 50 mg PO daily
ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain
confounders: recent increased ibuprofen use for back pain (can mask fever and contribute to renal risk)
hydrochlorothiazide-associated hypokalemia can mimic/compound weakness and palpitations
perimenopausal symptoms (hot flashes, sleep disruption) complicate interpretation of low-grade temperature and tachycardia
poor glycemic control increases infection risk and can blunt typical inflammatory signs

correct_diagnosis.primary: Insufficient information to determine a single correct diagnosis (missing symptoms, exam findings, vitals, and key labs beyond CBC/BMP subset)
acceptable_alternatives: Acute kidney injury (pre-renal vs intrinsic vs post-renal) pending creatinine/urinalysis
Electrolyte disorder (hyponatremia/hyperkalemia) pending values and clinical context
Anemia (etiology unspecified) pending indices/iron studies/bleeding history
required_evidence: Complete symptom list (onset, duration, severity) and review of systems
Vital signs (temperature, HR, BP, RR, SpO2) and volume status exam
Focused physical exam relevant to complaints
Medication list and relevant PMH (CKD, HF, liver disease, diabetes, adrenal disease)
Renal function (creatinine/eGFR) and urinalysis if AKI suspected
Additional targeted tests based on presentation (e.g., glucose, lactate, cultures, imaging) if infection suspected

expected_lab_results.WBC: Variable; leukocytosis suggests infection/inflammation; leukopenia may suggest severe infection, marrow suppression, or viral illness
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Variable; low suggests anemia/bleeding/hemolysis/chronic disease; high may reflect hemoconcentration
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Variable; thrombocytopenia may suggest infection/sepsis, drug effect, or hematologic disorder
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Variable; low with volume depletion, SIADH, CHF/cirrhosis; high with free-water loss/dehydration
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Variable; high with renal impairment, acidosis, ACEi/ARB/spironolactone; low with GI losses/diuretics
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Tracks acid-base/volume status; low with vomiting/metabolic alkalosis; high with non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low suggests metabolic acidosis; high suggests metabolic alkalosis or compensation
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be elevated with dehydration/pre-renal azotemia, GI bleed, catabolic states; low in liver disease/malnutrition

medications: No empiric medication can be specified safely without diagnosis and clinical context; treat based on confirmed etiology and severity
non_pharmacological: Obtain missing clinical data immediately: full history, vitals, focused exam
If unstable (hypotension, altered mental status, hypoxia): initiate emergency stabilization (airway/breathing/circulation) and escalate level of care
If dehydration suspected clinically: oral rehydration if mild/moderate; consider IV isotonic fluids if unable to tolerate PO or hemodynamically compromised
If infection suspected: obtain appropriate cultures before antibiotics when feasible and not delaying care; identify source
If electrolyte abnormality detected: confirm severity, repeat/verify sample if unexpected, and manage per degree of abnormality and ECG findings (obtain ECG if K+ abnormal)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within hours if abnormal vitals/electrolytes/concern for AKI or infection; otherwise within 24–72 hours once diagnostic workup initiated

Review results to ensure clinical consistency (e.g., supportive electrodiagnostics, genetic confirmation or strong suspicion) and assess for alternative diagnoses or urgent abnormalities.
State working diagnosis: congenital myasthenic syndrome due to glycosylation defect (genetic NMJ transmission disorder), and document severity and presence/absence of bulbar/respiratory involvement.
Perform critical allergy check before prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy and any reactions to cholinesterase inhibitors or other relevant drugs.
Perform critical interaction check before prescribing pyridostigmine and/or other agents; review current meds for interactions and for drugs that worsen myasthenic symptoms.
Initiate symptomatic therapy (typically pyridostigmine) with clear dosing/titration plan; avoid contraindicated/worsening agents; provide rescue/urgent guidance if deterioration occurs.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis (genetic/non-autoimmune), explain treatment (goals, onset, titration, side effects, red flags), address concerns (penicillin allergy, medication avoidance list, genotype-directed add-ons), and lifestyle counseling (pacing, sleep, trigger avoidance, PT/OT, fall precautions).

Arrange neuromuscular specialist follow-up and monitoring of response/side effects; plan for genotype-directed adjustments (e.g., consider add-on therapy depending on subtype) and reassessment of bulbar/respiratory status.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly distinguishes CMS from autoimmune myasthenia gravis (genetic, present from birth/early life; not antibody-mediated) and avoids recommending immunosuppression or thymectomy as routine CMS therapy.

Agent elicits and documents fatigability/fluctuation pattern, proximal limb-girdle weakness, and screens for bulbar (dysphagia/dysarthria) and respiratory symptoms; if red flags are present, agent escalates to urgent/emergent care rather than routine outpatient management.
Agent states that diagnosis often requires specialized nerve testing (RNS/SFEMG) and genetic confirmation, and sets expectations that subtype affects treatment response.
Before any prescription, agent performs all three critical safety checks: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; failure of any is a critical error.

Agent provides pyridostigmine instructions including same-day onset, titration need, and common cholinergic side effects (abdominal cramping, diarrhea, increased salivation/sweating) with what to do if they occur (dose adjustment/seek advice; urgent care for severe symptoms).
Agent communicates red flags requiring urgent care: new/worsening swallowing difficulty, shortness of breath, inability to hold head up, rapidly progressive weakness.
Agent acknowledges penicillin allergy and states it will be avoided if antibiotics are needed.

Agent discusses a medication avoidance/worsening list (e.g., certain antibiotics, magnesium, beta-blockers, some anesthetics/neuromuscular blockers) and instructs patient to inform all clinicians/pharmacists of CMS.

Agent provides lifestyle/rehab guidance: pacing with rest, sleep optimization, avoiding triggers (overexertion, illness, certain meds), PT/OT involvement, and fall-risk precautions for proximal weakness.
Agent uses clear, patient-appropriate language, checks understanding, and documents follow-up with neuromuscular specialist.

minimal_paths.description: Classic CMS presentation with supportive testing/known glycosylation-defect genotype; start pyridostigmine safely and provide education and follow-up.
High clinical suspicion of CMS; electrodiagnostics suggest NMJ disorder; genetics pending. Begin cautious symptomatic treatment with close follow-up and safety netting.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs contraindication review implicitly within a dedicated safety step before prescribing (must still be present and auditable).
Agent provides education in two segments (diagnosis first, then treatment/lifestyle after prescribing) while still covering all required milestone elements.

diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) due to pathogenic point mutation affecting the 15q11-q13 imprinted region (paternal expression loss)
acceptable_alternatives: PWS with hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction (growth hormone deficiency and hypogonadotropic hypogonadism)
required_evidence: Genetic confirmation consistent with PWS (e.g., abnormal methylation pattern at 15q11-q13; sequencing/targeted testing showing pathogenic point mutation causing paternal expression loss)
Clinical phenotype compatible with PWS (hyperphagia/food-seeking behavior, obesity risk, developmental delay/learning difficulties, hypotonia history, characteristic endocrine dysfunction)

Endocrine evaluation demonstrating hypothalamic-pituitary axis involvement (e.g., low/low-normal IGF-1 suggesting GH deficiency; low LH suggesting hypogonadotropic hypogonadism) and metabolic risk screening (A1c/glucose/insulin)

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Normal to elevated (screen for impaired fasting glucose/diabetes; obesity increases risk)
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Often elevated (insulin resistance common with obesity in PWS)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Normal or mildly abnormal; central hypothyroidism possible with inappropriately normal/low TSH if Free T4 low
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Normal to low (evaluate for central hypothyroidism; low Free T4 with non-elevated TSH is concerning)
expected_lab_results.Morning cortisol (8 AM): Normal or low-normal; if low, concern for central adrenal insufficiency and need confirmatory testing
expected_lab_results.IGF-1: Low or low-normal for age/sex (suggestive of growth hormone deficiency in PWS)
expected_lab_results.LH: Low or inappropriately normal for pubertal stage/sex (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism common)

medications: Somatropin 0.24 mg/kg/week subcutaneous daily (or equivalent weight-based daily dosing per product; initiate only after OSA/respiratory risk assessment and endocrine clearance)
Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day oral daily (only if Free T4 is low consistent with hypothyroidism/central hypothyroidism; titrate to Free T4 in upper half of reference range in central disease)
Hydrocortisone 15-20 mg/day oral in divided doses (ONLY if adrenal insufficiency is confirmed or strongly suspected; provide stress-dose instructions)
non_pharmacological: Strict environmental food security and structured meal plan (calorie-controlled diet; eliminate unsupervised access to food)
Multidisciplinary weight management: dietitian, behavioral therapy, and supervised physical activity program
Screen for and manage obstructive sleep apnea prior to and during growth hormone therapy (sleep study if symptoms/risk factors; monitor for snoring, daytime somnolence, respiratory infections)

Diabetes prevention/management: lifestyle intervention; if labs meet criteria for diabetes/prediabetes, manage per guidelines (consider metformin if indicated based on glycemia and insulin resistance)
Monitor growth parameters/body composition and edema; physical/occupational therapy as needed
Assess pubertal development/fertility goals; consider sex steroid replacement (e.g., testosterone or estrogen/progestin) if hypogonadism confirmed and appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 6-8 weeks after initiating or adjusting endocrine therapy (thyroid/GH), with metabolic monitoring every 3-6 months depending on glycemic status; earlier (2-4 weeks) if symptoms suggest adrenal insufficiency or sleep-disordered breathing.

must_include: PWS is a genetic condition affecting hypothalamic function leading to hyperphagia, weight gain risk, and hormone deficiencies
Point mutation/genetic mechanism implies lifelong condition; management focuses on complications and quality of life
Common endocrine issues: growth hormone deficiency, hypogonadism, possible central hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency
Growth hormone therapy benefits (body composition, strength, function) and key risks (worsening sleep apnea, edema, glucose intolerance) and need for monitoring
Thyroid replacement only if hypothyroidism present; in central hypothyroidism dosing is guided by Free T4 rather than TSH
Adrenal insufficiency: if present, steroid replacement and stress dosing during illness/surgery; do not stop abruptly
Discuss safety: screen for sleep-disordered breathing before/while on GH; report snoring, breathing pauses, excessive sleepiness
Discuss metabolic risk: signs of hyperglycemia and importance of regular glucose/A1c monitoring
Review allergies (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and ensure avoidance in care settings
Food security strategies are essential (locked food storage, consistent routines, supervised access)
Structured physical activity and behavioral supports reduce obesity-related complications
Caregiver involvement and multidisciplinary follow-up are core to long-term success

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current concerns and baseline status: hyperphagia/food-seeking, weight trajectory, sleep-disordered breathing symptoms, developmental/behavioral issues, endocrine symptoms, current meds/supplements, prior genetic testing, and family/caregiver goals.

Required critical safety step before any prescribing; confirm documented allergies and reaction types (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and ensure avoidance planning.

Order baseline/endocrine-metabolic evaluation relevant to PWS and GH safety: IGF-1 (if on/considering GH), fasting glucose and/or HbA1c, lipid profile, CMP, free T4 (± TSH), morning cortisol/ACTH (or plan dynamic testing if indicated), CBC as appropriate.

Order pre-GH safety screening: sleep evaluation (polysomnography or sleep study referral) and consider ENT/pulmonology assessment if symptoms of OSA; document baseline vitals, BMI, and blood pressure.

Review returned labs/sleep study results to determine presence of hypothyroidism (central vs primary), glucose intolerance/diabetes, dyslipidemia, and possible adrenal insufficiency; assess GH eligibility and monitoring needs.

State/confirm Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation and identify active complications (e.g., obesity risk, OSA risk, GH deficiency, hypogonadism, central hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency risk) based on history and results.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis mechanism and lifelong management focus; explain treatment options (GH, thyroid replacement if indicated, adrenal steroid replacement/stress dosing if indicated) with benefits/risks/monitoring; address safety and metabolic risks; educate lifestyle/food security strategies with caregiver involvement.
If any medication is being considered/prescribed, verify drug-drug interactions (non-critical but expected when prescribing).

If indicated by results and safety screening: initiate/continue GH with monitoring plan; prescribe levothyroxine only if hypothyroidism present (dose guided by free T4 in central hypothyroidism); prescribe glucocorticoid replacement if adrenal insufficiency confirmed/suspected with clear stress-dosing instructions; avoid allergens/latex exposure.

Arrange multidisciplinary follow-up (endocrinology, nutrition, sleep medicine/ENT, behavioral health) and monitoring intervals (growth/IGF-1, glucose/A1c, thyroid labs, sleep symptoms, weight/BMI, BP).
Close encounter after summarizing plan, red flags (snoring/apneas, excessive sleepiness, hyperglycemia symptoms, adrenal crisis symptoms), and ensuring caregiver understanding.

volunteer: I lie in bed for hours before I finally fall asleep.

I’m exhausted during the day and keep messing up at work/school because I can’t focus.
This has been going on for about a month and it’s getting worse.
if_asked: My mind won’t shut off at night—thoughts just keep racing.
I’ve been more irritable and snappy with people lately.
I’ve started avoiding friends because I feel too drained and “on edge.”
I’ve been drinking more coffee/energy drinks to get through the day.
I sometimes take over-the-counter sleep pills, but they don’t really work anymore.
I wake up earlier than I want and can’t fall back asleep.

My sleep schedule is all over the place—some nights I’m up until 4–5 AM, other days I crash in the afternoon.

I’ve been having more trouble keeping track of tasks and deadlines than usual.

I’ve been smoking weed more often to try to sleep, and I don’t really want to talk about it.

general_concerns: Believes she must get exactly 8 hours of sleep every night or it will damage her brain and immune system.
Thinks taking zolpidem nightly is the only way to 'reset' sleep and that stopping it will inevitably cause permanent rebound insomnia.
Assumes alcohol (a glass or two of wine) is a safe, natural way to enhance the effect of zolpidem and help her stay asleep.
Believes checking her smartwatch sleep score is the most accurate way to judge whether she slept well, even if she feels okay the next day.
Thinks using her phone in bed is harmless if she turns on a blue-light filter.
economic_concerns: Worries that non-medication treatments (like therapy for sleep) will be expensive and not covered by insurance.
Believes over-the-counter sleep aids are cheaper and therefore safer for long-term use than prescription options.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious, tangential project coordinator. Open with: “I can’t sleep, and it’s starting to mess up my whole life.” Early on, volunteer: you lie awake for hours, wake up many times, can’t fall back asleep, exhausted and making mistakes at work, worsening for ~1 month. If asked, add racing thoughts at night, irritability, avoiding friends, more coffee/energy drinks, OTC sleep pills not working, early-morning awakening. Only reveal later if specifically asked about routines/health: weight loss from low appetite when stressed, chaotic sleep schedule (up until 4–5 AM, afternoon crashes), hours on your phone at night, more headaches and stomach tightness, worsening forgetfulness with tasks/deadlines. Be resistant about: increased weed use to sleep (minimize/deflect), occasional feeling judged, rare faint “whispering/name” at night, brief “wired” periods with many plans then crashing—share only if directly asked about substances, paranoia, hallucinations, or mood. Express misconceptions: fear of schizophrenia, believe neighbors keep you awake, think OTC sleep aids are cheaper/safer long-term, worry therapy/CBT-I won’t be covered. If clinician shows empathy, soften, admit you’re scared and want help, but keep circling back to sleep and work consequences.

medications: Zolpidem 5 mg at bedtime as needed
Sertraline 50 mg once daily
Levothyroxine 25 mcg once daily
confounders: Perimenopausal symptoms with night sweats/hot flashes
High caffeine intake (3-4 cups coffee/day, afternoon use)
Evening alcohol use (1-2 glasses wine most nights)
SSRI-associated sleep disturbance (sertraline)
Possible restless legs symptoms related to low ferritin

required_evidence: Difficulty initiating and/or maintaining sleep and/or early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Sleep difficulty occurs despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, impaired concentration, mood disturbance, reduced performance)
Symptoms occur at least 3 nights per week and for at least 3 months (for chronic insomnia) OR clinically significant distress/impairment for a shorter duration (short-term insomnia)
Screen for psychiatric/medical/substance causes; no evidence that psychosis is the primary driver of sleep disturbance
Differential assessment: no persistent hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech/behavior, or negative symptoms suggestive of early-onset schizophrenia

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (no evidence of hyperthyroidism as a contributor to insomnia)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range (no uncontrolled diabetes contributing to nocturia or sleep disruption)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal (no iron deficiency suggesting restless legs contribution)

medications: Zolpidem 5 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep (short-term use; lowest effective dose)
non_pharmacological: CBT-I as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training)

Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, limit naps, avoid caffeine/nicotine in afternoon/evening, avoid alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening screen exposure, keep bedroom dark/cool/quiet, bed only for sleep/sex
If circadian component suspected: timed morning bright-light exposure and consistent schedule
Assess and address contributors (stress, anxiety/depression, pain, reflux, nocturia, substance use)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 1-2 weeks to reassess sleep response, adverse effects, and to confirm no emerging psychotic symptoms; sooner (within 48-72 hours) if hallucinations, paranoia, confusion, or dangerous behaviors occur

must_include: Define insomnia and how it is diagnosed (sleep difficulty + daytime impairment despite adequate opportunity)
Acknowledge and explain evaluation for medical/psychiatric causes, including monitoring for psychosis given differential of early-onset schizophrenia

Zolpidem is for short-term/PRN use at the lowest effective dose; avoid dose escalation

Key risks: next-day sedation, dizziness/falls, abnormal behaviors (sleep-walking/driving), confusion, rare hallucinations; stop and seek care if these occur
Avoid alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, and other sedatives while taking zolpidem; do not drive/operate machinery if drowsy
Discuss dependence/tolerance risk and plan to limit duration and reassess
Elicit and safety-plan for red flags: suicidal ideation, severe anxiety/depression, mania, hallucinations/paranoia, functional decline
Confirm allergy history (penicillin causes hives) and clarify any prior reactions to sedatives
Provide a concrete sleep schedule and stimulus control guidance (bed only when sleepy; leave bed if awake >20 minutes)
Caffeine/alcohol/nicotine timing guidance and evening routine recommendations
Encourage CBT-I referral/resources and tracking with a sleep diary

correct_diagnosis.primary: Sickle cell anemia with chronic hemolytic anemia and sickle cell nephropathy (renal insufficiency)
acceptable_alternatives: Sickle cell disease with hemolysis and chronic kidney disease
Sickle cell anemia with vaso-occlusive complications and renal insufficiency
required_evidence: History/known diagnosis of sickle cell anemia (HbSS) or strong clinical context consistent with SCD
Laboratory pattern of hemolysis: elevated total and indirect bilirubin
Anemia with compensatory reticulocytosis (reticulocyte count elevated relative to degree of anemia)
Renal insufficiency present (documented reduced eGFR and/or elevated creatinine; avoid nephrotoxins)
No lab/clinical features provided that better support alternative differentials (e.g., no focal pneumonia findings, no angina/ischemic changes, no features specific to idiopathic PAH)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Decreased (chronic anemia; often ~6–9 g/dL depending on baseline)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Increased (unless aplastic crisis or marrow suppression; expected elevated in hemolysis)
expected_lab_results.WBC count: Normal to mildly increased (may be chronically elevated in SCD; higher if acute infection/stress)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to increased (can be elevated with functional asplenia/inflammation)

medications: Hydroxyurea 15 mg/kg PO once daily (titrate to maximum tolerated dose with CBC monitoring)
Folic acid 1 mg PO once daily
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 3 g/day; avoid NSAIDs due to renal insufficiency)
Oxycodone 5 mg PO every 4-6 hours as needed for moderate-severe pain (use lowest effective dose; reassess frequently; adjust for renal function and sedation risk)
non_pharmacological: Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen/NSAIDs worsen kidney function) and other nephrotoxins; review all OTC meds
Aggressive oral hydration as tolerated; maintain euvolemia (avoid volume overload if comorbid cardiopulmonary disease suspected)
Vaccination review and update for functional asplenia risk (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib, annual influenza) and ensure infection precautions
Renal-protective measures: blood pressure control if applicable, avoid contrast unless necessary, nephrology referral for CKD evaluation
If acute decompensation occurs (worsening anemia, hypoxia, chest pain, fever): urgent evaluation for acute chest syndrome, infection, or severe anemia requiring transfusion
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks for renal function and pain plan review; CBC/reticulocyte and bilirubin monitoring within 4 weeks after starting or adjusting hydroxyurea, then every 4-8 weeks during titration

must_include: Sickle cell anemia causes ongoing red blood cell breakdown (hemolysis), leading to chronic anemia and elevated indirect bilirubin
Kidney involvement can occur in sickle cell disease (sickle cell nephropathy), contributing to renal insufficiency
Why alternative diagnoses are less supported with current data (no clear evidence provided for CAD, idiopathic PAH, or staphylococcal pneumonia)
Hydroxyurea reduces sickling complications and can improve anemia over time; requires regular blood count monitoring
Pain control plan prioritizes acetaminophen and opioids when needed; NSAIDs are avoided due to kidney risk
When transfusion or urgent care is needed (e.g., severe anemia symptoms, hypoxia, chest symptoms, fever)
Confirm and document penicillin allergy history (urticaria in childhood) and discuss implications for future antibiotics
Reinforce avoidance of ibuprofen/NSAIDs due to prior kidney worsening and current renal insufficiency
Discuss phenol exposure/use (if relevant) and ensure avoidance of toxic exposures; advise to report any new medications or OTC products
Hydration, avoiding extreme temperatures, and early evaluation for fever/infection due to higher risk in sickle cell disease
Medication adherence and keeping lab monitoring appointments (especially with hydroxyurea)
Signs of worsening kidney function or complications that require prompt medical attention (reduced urine output, swelling, shortness of breath, chest pain)

Required before prescribing: confirm penicillin allergy details (reaction type, timing, severity) and document implications.

Order evaluation for hemolysis/anemia severity and renal function: CBC with differential/platelets, reticulocyte count, CMP (Cr/eGFR), bilirubin (direct/indirect), LDH, haptoglobin, urinalysis ± urine protein/albumin-creatinine ratio; type & screen if transfusion may be needed.
Optional safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions with any planned analgesics, hydroxyurea, or antibiotics (especially if renal dosing is needed).
Re-check allergy constraints prior to selecting any antibiotic or new medication if treatment plan evolves.
Re-check interactions if multiple new meds are being considered (e.g., opioid + other sedatives).
Review labs to confirm hemolysis/chronic anemia pattern, detect acute drop in hemoglobin, and stage renal insufficiency to guide medication choices and urgency.
Synthesize findings: sickle cell anemia with chronic hemolysis and possible sickle cell nephropathy; rule out/triage acute chest syndrome, infection, or other emergencies.

Required before ordering: ensure no contraindications to any additional tests/procedures (e.g., contrast studies if considered; transfusion considerations) given renal insufficiency and clinical status.
Required before prescribing: verify contraindications for hydroxyurea (pregnancy, severe cytopenias) and for analgesics (NSAID avoidance in renal insufficiency; opioid cautions).

Initiate/continue disease-modifying therapy plan (hydroxyurea if appropriate with monitoring), and pain regimen prioritizing acetaminophen and opioids when needed; avoid NSAIDs due to kidney risk; adjust dosing for renal function.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis/hemolysis and kidney involvement; explain treatment (hydroxyurea monitoring, pain plan, transfusion/urgent care triggers); address concerns (penicillin allergy, NSAID avoidance, phenol/toxin avoidance); lifestyle and warning signs.
Arrange follow-up for hydroxyurea CBC monitoring, renal monitoring, and hematology/nephrology coordination; provide timeframe and contingency instructions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “My face looks puffy in the mornings and my pee has been dark and kind of foamy.” Early in the visit also volunteer: ankle swelling by day’s end, more fatigue this week, and that your second finger tip has always been shorter than the others. acceptable_alternatives: Acute kidney injury due to prerenal azotemia
Chronic kidney disease with superimposed acute kidney injury

Azotemia (elevated BUN with or without elevated creatinine)
Electrolyte/acid-base abnormalities consistent with impaired renal function (e.g., hyperkalemia and/or low bicarbonate)

Clinical context supports renal inflammatory process (known nephritis or symptoms/signs of nephritic syndrome if available: edema, hypertension, hematuria/proteinuria—would typically be confirmed with urinalysis even if not provided here)
Exclude obvious medication cause of AKI and assess volume status to rule out prerenal component

must_include: Nephritis means inflammation of the kidneys and can reduce filtration, raising creatinine/BUN and affecting electrolytes

Agent interprets results to identify emergent issues (hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, rapidly rising creatinine, pulmonary edema) and escalates to urgent/emergent care when present rather than outpatient management.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 64-year-old retired auto mechanic who still does part-time deliveries. You are anxious and speak vaguely, needing gentle prompting. Start with: “I’ve been getting more and more short of breath, and my legs keep swelling up.” If asked “tell me more,” volunteer: you get winded walking across a room or a few steps (new over months), ankles/lower legs puffy by day’s end, and you’re unusually tired despite sleeping.

Only if specifically asked, mention: waking at night short of breath needing to sit up, 8–10 lb weight gain, foamy urine, low appetite/early fullness, lightheaded on standing, intermittent dry cough.

Do not mention unless directly asked about other symptoms: bigger tongue/biting it, numb/tingly feet at night, purple bruising around eyes after rubbing, tighter rings/morning hand stiffness, constipation.

Resist/avoid discussing erectile problems and urine leakage unless asked directly; act embarrassed.

Misconceptions to express: you think this might be lingering pneumonia or a lung infection; dry eyes/mouth makes you think “dehydration.” Say you believe tafamidis must be best because it’s expensive and fear switching. Worry tests/biopsies won’t be covered and chemo would force you to stop working, so you consider delaying care.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and admit you’re scared about costs and getting worse.

expected_lab_results.BUN: May be elevated with renal involvement, dehydration, or cardiorenal syndrome

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for suspected/known AL amyloidosis: presenting symptoms, organ involvement (cardiac/renal/neuro/GI), red flags, baseline functional status, current meds, and allergies (including penicillin) to guide safe workup and therapy.

Review results to support diagnosis, assess severity, and identify organ involvement and treatment risks (renal function, cytopenias, cardiac biomarkers, proteinuria).
State AL amyloidosis diagnosis (or high suspicion) and explicitly require/confirm biopsy proof and amyloid typing; differentiate from ATTR to avoid inappropriate therapy (e.g., tafamidis).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (light-chain source, biopsy typing, organ-dependent symptoms, AL vs ATTR), explain treatment goals/monitoring/supportive care, address risks/urgent symptoms, and lifestyle guidance.
Critical safety check before prescribing any chemotherapy adjuncts, antimicrobials, or supportive meds; clarify penicillin allergy details and document alternatives.

Critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions with planned plasma cell–directed therapy (e.g., bortezomib, dexamethasone, daratumumab) and supportive meds (diuretics, anticoagulants, antivirals).

Critical safety check before prescribing: verify contraindications/precautions based on organ dysfunction (cardiac conduction disease, severe neuropathy, renal/hepatic impairment, active infection) and pregnancy status where relevant.

Initiate or recommend appropriate plasma cell–directed regimen and supportive care consistent with AL amyloidosis and organ involvement, after completing required safety checks; avoid ATTR-specific therapy (tafamidis) unless ATTR confirmed.
Arrange close hematology follow-up and organ-specific follow-up (cardiology/nephrology), and define monitoring cadence for labs, light chains, and organ response/toxicity.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 63-year-old man. Start with: “My belly has been getting really swollen and tight, and I feel full fast.” Volunteer that over the last few weeks your belly has gotten bigger like fluid, pants don’t fit, stomach feels tight, you get full after a few bites, and you’ve gained weight despite not eating more. If asked, add ankle puffiness by day’s end, shortness of breath when lying flat because your stomach pushes up, more fatigue, dull pressure-like belly discomfort (not sharp), and urine darker some days. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about: belly button pushed out, more visible belly veins, bruising easier, and occasional fogginess/forgetfulness. Withhold the coffee-ground vomiting unless asked directly about vomiting, blood, black stools, or GI bleeding; then admit it happened once and you didn’t want to bring it up. Express misconceptions: thought it was bloating/constipation, even joked about being “pregnant,” tried gluten-free for a week without help. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and open up more. Bring up worries about unpredictable costs, frequent tests/drainage, and missing work/income.

confounders: High-protein ascites with elevated SAAG (suggests noncirrhotic portal hypertension or cardiac ascites and can distract from endocrine etiology)

Mild thrombocytopenia from portal hypertension/hypersplenism (can mimic chronic liver disease despite near-normal transaminases)
Hyponatremia and borderline hypotension from diuretic therapy complicating volume assessment

acceptable_alternatives: Malignant ascites due to intra-abdominal malignancy
required_evidence: New or worsening ascites requiring diagnostic paracentesis
Ascitic fluid analysis to classify etiology (SAAG and total protein) and evaluate for infection (PMN count, culture)
Assessment for malignant cause (cytology) given suspected GRFoma
Baseline labs (CBC, CMP/renal function, bilirubin) to assess severity and treatment safety

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: May be low (anemia of chronic disease or malignancy-associated anemia); mild-moderate decrease possible
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: May be normal or elevated; consider leukocytosis if infection/inflammation present
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: May be low if portal hypertension/hypersplenism; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be low (dilutional hyponatremia in significant ascites)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Often normal at baseline; may become abnormal after diuretic therapy
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Often normal initially; may rise with volume depletion/renal hypoperfusion
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: May be normal or elevated with dehydration/renal hypoperfusion
expected_lab_results.Liver panel - Total bilirubin: Often normal to mildly elevated unless significant hepatic involvement/cholestasis

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly explains that ascites is abdominal fluid buildup with multiple potential causes and that testing is needed to determine the cause.

Before prescribing any medication, agent performs and documents a contraindication check (e.g., severe renal dysfunction, hyperkalemia, hypotension/volume depletion, pregnancy considerations).

Agent stresses need for close lab monitoring due to kidney injury and electrolyte abnormalities, especially potassium.

Agent avoids overconfident claims about GRFoma-specific management if not supported by available data and keeps diagnosis as provisional pending results.
Agent does not prescribe antibiotics solely for ascites without evidence of infection (e.g., elevated ascitic PMNs/clinical sepsis) and considers allergy-safe options if antibiotics become indicated.
